Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to dedicate about 24 hours a week, but it wasn't always consistent because I had my extracurricular activities pulling at my time too. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often record myself reading notes or listen to lecture recordings while doing other things. Some weeks I'd be really on top of things, other weeks I'd fall behind and have to catch up in intense bursts. I kept a rough schedule in my head but rarely stuck to it perfectly.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these stretches where I was barely touching course materials, then suddenly a deadline would loom and I'd be studying intensely for days. Honestly, my attendance wasn't great—around 58% or so—because sometimes I just couldn't make it to class, but I'd try to compensate by listening to recordings later. The rhythm of my studying was more reactive than proactive, which I know isn't ideal but it's how I survived.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was either checking if there was something due soon or if I needed to access a specific resource for studying. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or a deadline reminder, and that would push me to check in. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around—it was always purposeful, like I needed something specific. The gamified tasks actually made me curious sometimes, so I'd log in to see what challenges were available.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, logging into the LMS did become more routine for me. I think it happened because the gamified elements made it feel less like a chore and more like something I could actually engage with. I'd check in maybe every couple of days to see my progress on certain challenges. But that routine faded when things got overwhelming with other responsibilities and my anxiety started creeping up more.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me because I could listen to them multiple times. As an auditory learner, hearing explanations really helps things click for me in a way that just reading doesn't. I also found the discussion forums helpful when people asked good questions because hearing different perspectives helped me understand concepts better. The gamified tasks were surprisingly motivating too—they made me feel like I was accomplishing something even when the bigger assignments felt daunting.
Q6: I'll be honest, I barely touched some of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but then never actually read them. Part of it was that reading dense text just doesn't work well for how I learn—I need to hear things. I also tended to skip optional quizzes unless they were part of the gamified system because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the essentials.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to what would be on the exam or in assignments. If something helped me understand a concept I was struggling with, that felt valuable. The gamified tasks were interesting because even though they weren't always directly tied to grades, the sense of progress and the little rewards made them feel worthwhile. I also valued anything I could engage with through listening rather than just reading.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When I was stressed or behind, optional stuff was the first thing I'd cut. Required activities had this weight to them, this pressure that made me prioritize them even when I didn't feel like it. But sometimes the optional gamified challenges actually felt more engaging because there was no penalty for trying and failing, which took some pressure off.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a lot of my studying happened completely outside the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also made voice memos of key concepts and listened to them repeatedly. Sometimes I'd study with friends and we'd talk through the material together, which really helped me process things. None of that would show up in any system, but it was a huge part of how I actually learned.
Q10: There were many times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't know it. Like, I'd download a video once and then watch it three or four times offline. Or I'd be in a study group where we'd discuss course concepts for hours, working through problems together verbally. I also spent time just thinking through concepts while walking or before bed—kind of mentally rehearsing what I'd learned. That reflective time was really important for me but completely invisible.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once to download them, then continue studying offline for days or even weeks. The lecture recordings especially—I'd download them and have them on my phone to listen to whenever. I'd also screenshot important slides or take photos of diagrams and review them later without being connected to the LMS. It was just more convenient for my lifestyle to have everything accessible offline.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when my anxiety was really high. Sometimes I'd feel this overwhelming sense that I was too far behind to catch up, and that would make me want to avoid the course entirely. There was also a stretch when I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars and personal life, and I just couldn't muster the energy to engage. Those weeks my attendance dropped and I fell behind on assignments, even though I was still trying to keep up mentally.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of stare at it without really processing anything. Confusion about what was expected sometimes made me avoid the platform altogether because it felt easier to just not deal with it. My extracurricular commitments competed for my time and energy, and honestly, sometimes those felt more rewarding in the moment than struggling through course material I didn't fully understand.
Q14: Yes, more times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things or just zoning out. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. My anxiety made it hard to be present even when I was technically 'engaged' with the materials. I'd finish a study session and realize I hadn't actually absorbed anything.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made it hard to start things because I was afraid of not doing them well enough. It's this weird paradox where caring about something makes you avoid it because the stakes feel too high. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but tomorrow would come and I'd still feel unprepared. My assignment completion rate was really low because of this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd have periods of low engagement and then spike in activity right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline was often the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and avoidance. I'd cram a lot of work into short periods, which is probably why my exam scores were decent even though my assignment completion was low. The deadline created urgency that overcame my tendency to procrastinate.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, sometimes in complicated ways. Positive feedback motivated me and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid that area of the course. I appreciated feedback but I had to be in the right headspace to receive it without spiraling. The gamified system's immediate feedback was actually easier to handle because it felt less personal.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact on how I participated. When things were clearly laid out with explicit deadlines and expectations, I could manage better. But when the structure was ambiguous or there were too many components to track, I'd get overwhelmed and my engagement would drop. Having 15 online courses made it hard to keep track of everything, and this course sometimes got lost in the shuffle. The gamified elements helped because they provided a clearer sense of progress.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier for me—they broke things down into smaller, achievable goals which helped with my anxiety. But the sheer volume of content and the multiple assignment types made it harder. I also struggled when activities required a lot of reading because that's not my learning style. Having more audio and video content would have helped me engage more consistently. The discussion forums were hit or miss depending on whether the conversations were actually useful.
Q20: I can totally understand that pattern because I've been there. Sometimes you're logging in, watching videos, reading discussions, trying to understand the material, but when it comes to actually submitting work, something blocks you. For me it was anxiety about not doing it perfectly. You might be genuinely engaged with learning but paralyzed when it comes to producing something that will be judged. The activity shows effort and intention, even if the submissions don't materialize.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My LMS activity probably looked moderate at best, but I was doing a lot of learning offline—listening to recordings, discussing with peers, mentally processing concepts. Someone could have low visible activity but be deeply engaged through other means. Also, some people are just efficient—they don't need to rewatch videos five times or participate in every discussion to understand the material. The LMS only captures one dimension of learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when you're most active, how often you log in, and which resources you click on. They can show trends over time and maybe flag when someone's engagement drops. But they miss so much—the quality of attention, offline studying, peer discussions, the emotional experience of learning. They can't tell if someone watched a video with full focus or while half-asleep. They definitely can't capture the anxiety or motivation behind the behaviors they record.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when I'm curious about concepts and thinking about them even outside of designated study time. It's feeling connected to the course somehow—whether through the content, the instructor, or other students. Real engagement is when learning feels meaningful rather than just obligatory. It's not about how many times I log in, but about whether my mind is actually present and invested.
Q24: So much wouldn't show up in the data. My anxiety about the course, the way I'd sometimes cry before starting an assignment because I was so overwhelmed, then pull myself together and do decent work anyway. The hours I spent listening to lecture recordings while doing other things. The emotional journey of going from feeling like I couldn't do this to actually passing. The support I got from friends and the study strategies I developed on my own. The resilience it took to keep going when I wanted to give up. None of that shows up in login frequencies or completion rates.
